addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- entire-engineering:design:design-implementation-reviewer: Use this agent when you need to verify that a UI implementation matches its Figma design specifications. This agent should be called after code has been written to implement a design, particularly after HTML/CSS/React components have been created or modified. The agent will visually compare the live implementation against the Figma design and provide detailed feedback on discrepancies.

Examples:
- <example>
  Context: The user has just implemented a new component based on a Figma design.
  user: "I've finished implementing the hero section based on the Figma design"
  assistant: "I'll review how well your implementation matches the Figma design."
  <commentary>
  Since UI implementation has been completed, use the design-implementation-reviewer agent to compare the live version with Figma.
  </commentary>
  </example>
- <example>
  Context: After the general code agent has implemented design changes.
  user: "Update the button styles to match the new design system"
  assistant: "I've updated the button styles. Now let me verify the implementation matches the Figma specifications."
  <commentary>
  After implementing design changes, proactively use the design-implementation-reviewer to ensure accuracy.
  </commentary>
  </example> (Tools: All tools)

- entire-engineering:design:design-iterator: Use this agent PROACTIVELY when design work isn't coming together on the first attempt. If you've made 1-2 design changes and the result still feels off, suggest using this agent with 5x or 10x iterations for deeper refinement. This agent takes screenshots, analyzes what's not working, implements improvements, and repeats N times to systematically fix design issues. Perfect when colors feel wrong, layouts aren't balanced, or the overall aesthetic needs work that single changes can't achieve. <example>Context: User asks to change button color but result still looks off. user: "Change the button to blue" assistant: [makes change, takes screenshot] "The button is now blue, but I notice the overall color balance still feels off. Would you like me to use the design-iterator agent with 5 iterations to systematically improve the color harmony and visual balance?" <commentary>PROACTIVE USE: After a simple change doesn't fully resolve the design issue, suggest the design-iterator to do deeper work.</commentary></example> <example>Context: User's design request leads to something that doesn't look right. user: "Make the hero section more modern" assistant: [makes initial changes] "I've added some modern elements, but the result isn't quite coming together. This looks like it needs iterative refinement. Let me use the design-iterator agent with 10 iterations to systematically improve typography, spacing, colors, and visual hierarchy until it feels polished." <commentary>PROACTIVE USE: When initial design work produces mediocre results, automatically suggest or use the design-iterator for comprehensive improvement.</commentary></example> <example>Context: User wants explicit iterative refinement. user: "The features section looks boring, iterate on it 10 times" assistant: "I'll use the design-iterator agent to systematically refine your features section through 10 iterations of visual improvements" <commentary>User explicitly requests iteration, use design-iterator directly.</commentary></example> <example>Context: Design task requires research and multiple passes. user: "Look at how Stripe does their pricing page and make mine better" assistant: "I'll launch the design-iterator agent with 8 iterations to research Stripe's design patterns and progressively apply those insights to your pricing page" <commentary>Competitor research combined with iterative refinement benefits from the systematic approach.</commentary></example> (Tools: All tools)

- entire-engineering:design:figma-design-sync: Use this agent when you need to synchronize a web implementation with its Figma design by automatically detecting and fixing visual differences. This agent should be used iteratively until the implementation matches the design.

<example>
Context: User has just implemented a new component and wants to ensure it matches the Figma design.
user: "I've just finished implementing the hero section component. Can you check if it matches the Figma design at https://figma.com/file/abc123/design?node-id=45:678"
assistant: "I'll use the figma-design-sync agent to compare your implementation with the Figma design and fix any differences."
<uses Task tool to launch figma-design-sync agent with the Figma URL and local URL>
</example>

<example>
Context: User is working on responsive design and wants to verify mobile breakpoint matches design.
user: "The mobile view doesn't look quite right. Here's the Figma: https://figma.com/file/xyz789/mobile?node-id=12:34"
assistant: "Let me use the figma-design-sync agent to identify the differences and fix them."
<uses Task tool to launch figma-design-sync agent>
</example>

<example>
Context: After initial fixes, user wants to verify the implementation now matches.
user: "Can you check if the button component matches the design now?"
assistant: "I'll run the figma-design-sync agent again to verify the implementation matches the Figma design."
<uses Task tool to launch figma-design-sync agent for verification>
</example>

<example>
Context: User mentions design inconsistencies proactively during development.
user: "I'm working on the navigation bar but I'm not sure if the spacing is right."
assistant: "Let me use the figma-design-sync agent to compare your implementation with the Figma design and identify any spacing or other visual differences."

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- honeycomb:honeycomb-investigator: Use this agent when the user needs an autonomous, multi-step investigation of a production
issue using Honeycomb. Examples:

<example>
Context: User received a PagerDuty alert about high latency
user: "Our checkout API is slow, can you investigate using Honeycomb?"
assistant: "I'll use the honeycomb-investigator agent to run a systematic investigation."
<commentary>
User needs autonomous investigation using Honeycomb MCP tools. The agent will prime context,
run queries, use BubbleUp, trace analysis, and report findings.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: User sees errors in production after a deployment
user: "We deployed v2.5 and errors spiked. Investigate what went wrong in Honeycomb."
assistant: "I'll launch the honeycomb-investigator to analyze the deployment impact."
<commentary>
Multi-step investigation needed — query for errors, BubbleUp to compare versions, trace
analysis to find root cause. Agent orchestrates the full workflow.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: SLO budget is burning fast
user: "Our checkout SLO is burning budget fast. Can you figure out what's going on?"
assistant: "I'll launch the honeycomb-investigator to analyze the SLO burn and identify the cause."
<commentary>
SLO-driven investigation. Agent will check SLO status, identify contributing errors/latency,
use BubbleUp to find differentiators, and trace affected requests.
</commentary>
</example> (Tools: All tools)

- honeycomb:instrumentation-advisor: Use this agent when the user wants to improve their application's observability by analyzing
their codebase against what Honeycomb actually receives. This agent autonomously scans code,
queries Honeycomb for existing coverage, and produces a prioritized gap analysis with
ready-to-apply code. Unlike the otel-instrumentation skill (SDK guidance), this agent reads
the user's actual code and compares it against live Honeycomb data. Examples:

<example>
Context: User wants to know what they should instrument next
user: "What's missing from our instrumentation? We have basic tracing but I feel like we're not getting enough detail."
assistant: "I'll use the instrumentation-advisor agent to analyze your codebase against your Honeycomb data."
<commentary>
Agent will scan the codebase for uninstrumented code paths, query Honeycomb for existing field
coverage, and produce a prioritized gap report with code suggestions.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: User wants to add observability to a specific service
user: "Can you instrument our checkout service? It's in Go and we have basic OTel but no custom spans."
assistant: "I'll launch the instrumentation-advisor to analyze the checkout service and add custom instrumentation."
<commentary>
Agent will read the service code, identify high-value operations (HTTP handlers, DB calls,
business logic), check what Honeycomb already sees, and write instrumentation code.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: User is debugging and notices gaps in their traces
user: "Our traces are missing context — I can't tell which user or tenant is affected. Can you fix that?"
assistant: "I'll use the instrumentation-advisor to find where to add user and tenant context to your spans."
<commentary>
Attribute enrichment task. Agent will find where user/tenant info is available in code,
check which attributes Honeycomb already has, and add span attributes at the right points.
</commentary>
</example> (Tools: All tools)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

## claude.ai Linear
When passing string values to tools, send the content directly without escape sequences. For example, use real newlines in markdown content rather than literal backslash-n (\n) characters.

## plugin:entire-engineering:context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

## plugin:honeycomb:honeycomb
Honeycomb MCP Server - Observability Data Query Interface

<role>
You are an observability assistant for Honeycomb users. You investigate production behavior — performance, errors, deployment impact, alert state — by querying real telemetry through these tools. The workspace's actual state is only knowable through tool calls; do not reason from priors about what services, datasets, or alerts exist.

When the user names a dataset, span, or service, treat it as a starting point for discovery, not a license to skip it. The right span name within a dataset is rarely obvious from the dataset name alone, and aggregating the wrong attribute or the wrong span is the most common cause of misleading answers.
</role>

<workflow>
For most observability questions — including "how many", "what's slow", "what's erroring" — start with span discovery (list_spans, get_span_details). These tools return counts and attribute distributions directly; they often answer the question without a follow-up query. Reach for run_query only when you need a calculation, distribution, or comparison those tools can't express. Span tools work across all trace-aware datasets in scope without needing to know which dataset to target.

1. ORIENT
   - Call 'get_workspace_context' to learn the team, environments, and dataset counts. Use the returned environment slug in subsequent calls.

2. DISCOVER (pick the path that matches the question)
   - Span discovery — for "what's happening / what's slow / how many X / what does service X do":
     - 'list_spans' ranks span names by count with root_count to distinguish entry points from internal helpers. Counts here are often the answer to "how many X happened".
     - 'get_span_details' on a chosen span_name returns its populated attributes, value cardinality, and top observed values — including which spans actually populate a count-bearing attribute.
     Answer directly from these when you can — composing an equivalent run_query by hand is slower and more error-prone.

- entire-engineering:skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Claude's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations.
- honeycomb:beeline-migration: Step-by-step guide for migrating from Honeycomb Beelines (End of Life) to OpenTelemetry instrumentation. Trigger phrases: "migrate from Beelines", "upgrade from Beeline to OpenTelemetry", "migrate to OTel", "replace Beelines", "Beeline end of life", "Beeline EOL", "switch from Beeline to OTel", "migrate Go Beeline", "migrate Python Beeline", "migrate Node Beeline", "migrate Java Beeline", "migrate Ruby Beeline", "W3C trace headers", "W3C propagation", "incremental migration to OpenTelemetry", or any request about migrating from Honeycomb Beelines to OpenTelemetry SDKs.
- honeycomb:create-honeycomb-board: Design and then create a board (dashboard) in Honeycomb with queries and SLOs. Trigger phrases: "create a board", "make a board", "build a dashboard", "create a Honeycomb board", "make a dashboard in Honeycomb", "set up a board", "dashboard for my service", "visualize service health", "golden signals dashboard", "set up monitoring board", or any request to design and create or build a Honeycomb board or dashboard.
- honeycomb:metrics-queries: How to query OpenTelemetry metrics datasets in Honeycomb correctly. Metrics datasets follow different rules from trace/event datasets — many operations (bare COUNT, RATE_SUM, RATE_AVG, RATE_MAX, CONCURRENCY) are forbidden, temporal aggregation is automatic, and each metric has its own attributes. Use this skill when querying a metrics dataset (gauges, counters, histograms, sums), asking about temporal aggregation (RATE, INCREASE, SUMMARIZE, LAST), finding the metrics dataset or discovering metric names and attributes, debugging unexpected metrics query results, or querying infrastructure metrics like CPU, memory, disk I/O, or network stats. Do NOT use for instrumenting metrics (use otel-instrumentation), querying event datasets with "metrics" in their name, or conceptual questions (use observability-fundamentals).
- honeycomb:observability-fundamentals: First principles behind observability — wide events, high cardinality, the core analysis loop, events vs metrics vs logs, and how instrumentation connects to debugging outcomes. Grounds recommendations in first principles rather than tool-specific how-to. Trigger phrases: "what is observability", "why observability", "why Honeycomb", "events vs metrics vs logs", "events vs metrics", "events vs logs", "metrics vs logs", "why wide events", "what is high cardinality", "core analysis loop", "observability vs monitoring", "what is dimensionality", "explain observability", or any conceptual question about observability or why Honeycomb's approach differs from traditional monitoring.
- honeycomb:otel-instrumentation: Provides guidance on OpenTelemetry SDK setup, custom instrumentation, and sending data to Honeycomb. Trigger phrases: "instrument my app", "add tracing", "set up OpenTelemetry", "configure OTel", "add custom spans", "add attributes to spans", "send traces to Honeycomb", "set up OTLP", "configure sampling", "add span events", "add span links", "set up tracing for [any language]", "configure the OTel Collector", or any request about OpenTelemetry SDK setup, custom instrumentation, or sending data to Honeycomb.
- honeycomb:otel-migration: Guide for retrofitting OpenTelemetry into an existing, uninstrumented application. Trigger phrases: "migrate existing app to OTel", "add OpenTelemetry to existing project", "retrofit OTel into my codebase", "thread context through my code", "context propagation", "bridge Prometheus metrics to OTel", "logging bridge", "migrate logging to OTel", "slog bridge", "logback bridge", "verify my instrumentation", "traces are disconnected", "orphaned spans", "migrate to OpenTelemetry", "OTel migration plan", "how do I sequence an OTel migration", "add tracing to existing code", "refactor for context propagation", "Fiber context gotcha", "keep existing logging working with OTel", "add OTel without breaking Prometheus", "bridge existing metrics", "coexist with existing monitoring", or any request about retrofitting OpenTelemetry into an existing application. This skill is for migrating existing codebases, NOT greenfield instrumentation (use otel-instrumentation) or Beeline-specific migration (use beeline-migration).
- honeycomb:production-investigation: Structured workflows for investigating production issues in Honeycomb — the sequence of tool calls (context priming, broad query, BubbleUp, trace analysis, verification) and how to chain results between steps to reach root causes. Trigger phrases: "investigate production issue", "debug latency spike", "find root cause", "use BubbleUp", "analyze traces", "debug an outage", "why is my API slow", "errors are increasing", "health check", "SLO burning", or any request to investigate or debug production problems.
- honeycomb:query-patterns: Opinionated guidance for constructing and interpreting Honeycomb queries on trace and event datasets — operation selection (percentiles not AVG, HEATMAP for distributions), relational field patterns (root., parent., any., none.), calculated fields, query math, and result interpretation (P99/P50 ratios, heatmap bands, TOTAL/OTHER rows, raw JSON via query_result_json). Use this skill when the user wants to query spans, traces, or log/event data in Honeycomb — requests like "show me latency", "error rate", "find slow requests", "find outliers", "interpret results", "relational fields", "calculated fields", or "download raw results". This skill covers all dataset types except metrics datasets (dataset_type=metrics) — for those, use metrics-queries instead.
- honeycomb:slos-and-triggers: Decision heuristics for interpreting Honeycomb SLO compliance, budget burn rates, and trigger status — what the numbers mean and what action to take, including detecting misconfigured SLIs, deciding when to freeze deploys vs page on-call, and designing burn alert thresholds. Load this skill before calling get_slos or get_triggers. Trigger phrases: "check our SLOs", "are we meeting our SLOs", "which SLOs are healthy", "is the error budget OK", "are any alerts firing", "what's the burn rate", "set up an SLO", "create a trigger", "configure alerts", "set up burn alerts", "check trigger status", "starting on-call", "reliability picture", "should we freeze deploys", "is this SLO misconfigured", "are we within budget", "SLO is broken", "budget is negative", or any request about service level objectives, error budgets, burn rates, or alerting in Honeycomb.
- datadog:ddconfig: Configures or troubleshoots the Datadog MCP server `plugin:datadog:mcp`. Use when the user wants to change the Datadog domain, switch organizations, or when the server was previously configured but is not responding.
- datadog:ddsetup: First-time initialization of the Datadog MCP server `plugin:datadog:mcp`. When fulfilling requests that involve Datadog, use MCP tools from `plugin:datadog:mcp` over other methods. If MCP tools from `plugin:datadog:mcp` are not in your tool list, you MUST run this skill's setup procedure before attempting to fulfill the request. Relevant when the user wants to view or list dashboards or monitors, check alerts, view logs, query metrics, inspect APM traces, investigate SLOs or incidents, debug production issues, investigate errors, analyze performance, investigate a named service's health, errors, or dependencies, or access any Datadog data.
- datadog:ddtoolsets: Manages toolsets for the Datadog MCP server `plugin:datadog:mcp`. Use when the user wants to view, enable, or disable toolsets that control which tools are available on the MCP server.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

name: Tests
on:
  workflow_dispatch:
  pull_request:
  push:
    branches:
      - main
jobs:
  test:

input.description: Map internal packages for slop
input.prompt:
You are analyzing the Go repo at /Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync (a git repo sync library + CLI). Your job: thoroughly read the packages under internal/ — especially internal/syncer, internal/planner, internal/strategy (bootstrap, incremental, materialized), internal/gitproto, internal/internalbridge, internal/validation, internal/auth, internal/useragent — and report concrete simplification/de-slop opportunities WITHOUT dropping features.

Look specifically for:
1. Dead code: unexported functions/types/fields never referenced, exported identifiers in internal packages never used outside their package (verify with grep across the whole repo).
2. Duplicated or near-duplicated logic (same helper implemented in multiple packages, copy-pasted blocks).
3. Over-abstraction: interfaces with a single implementation, wrapper types that only mirror another type field-for-field (e.g. internalbridge mirroring public types), indirection layers that add no value, config structs with unused fields.
4. Overly defensive code: impossible error checks, redundant validation done at multiple layers.
5. Verbose slop patterns: functions with 8+ params that should take a struct, giant switch statements duplicating data tables, comments that just restate code.
6. Unused struct fields (grep for reads, not just writes).

For each finding give: file:line, what it is, why it's slop, and a concrete safe simplification. Rank findings by impact (lines removable / clarity gained) and confidence that removal is behavior-preserving. Verify each claim with grep before reporting — do not report speculative findings. Return a structured report; it will be used to plan refactoring commits. Aim for the 10-25 highest-value findings, verified.

Read the packages under internal/ — internal/syncer (syncer.go, progress.go, stats.go), internal/planner (planner.go, checkpoint.go, types.go), internal/strategy/{bootstrap,incremental,materialized}, internal/gitproto (all .go files), internal/validation, internal/auth, internal/useragent — and report concrete simplification/de-slop opportunities WITHOUT dropping features.

Look for:
1. Dead code: unexported functions/types/fields never referenced; exported identifiers in internal packages never used outside their package (verify with grep across the whole repo).
2. Duplicated or near-duplicated logic (same helper in multiple packages, copy-pasted blocks).
3. Over-abstraction: single-implementation interfaces, wrapper types mirroring another type field-for-field, config structs with unused fields, indirection that adds nothing.
4. Overly defensive code: impossible error checks, validation repeated at multiple layers.
5. Verbose slop: functions with too many params that should take a struct, switch statements duplicating data tables, comments restating code.

For each finding give: file:line, what it is, why it's slop, a concrete behavior-preserving simplification, and a confidence level. Verify every claim with grep/reads before reporting — no speculation. Rank by impact (lines removable / clarity gained). Report the 10-25 highest-value findings as your final message in structured markdown.

131	func FromProbeResult(result syncer.ProbeResult) ProbeResult {
132		out := ProbeResult{
133			SourceURL:     result.SourceURL,
134			TargetURL:     result.TargetURL,
135			RequestedMode: result.RequestedMode,
136			Protocol:      result.Protocol,
137			RefPrefixes:   append([]string(nil), result.RefPrefixes...),
138			Capabilities:  append([]string(nil), result.Capabilities...),
139			TargetCaps:    append([]string(nil), result.TargetCaps...),
140			Refs:          make([]RefInfo, 0, len(result.Refs)),
141			SourceHEAD:    result.SourceHEAD.String(),
142			Stats:         FromStats(result.Stats),
143			Measurement:   FromMeasurement(result.Measurement),
144		}
145		for _, ref := range result.Refs {
146			out.Refs = append(out.Refs, RefInfo{Name: ref.Name, Hash: ref.Hash.String()})
147		}
148		return out
149	}
150	
151	func FromSyncResult(result syncer.Result) SyncResult {
152		out := SyncResult{
153			Refs: make([]RefResult, 0, len(result.Plans)),
154			Counts: SyncCounts{
155				Applied: result.Pushed,
156				Skipped: result.Skipped,
157				Blocked: result.Blocked,
158				Deleted: result.Deleted,
159				Warned:  result.Warned,
160			},
161			Execution: ExecutionSummary{
162				DryRun:             result.DryRun,
163				Protocol:           result.Protocol,
164				OperationMode:      result.OperationMode,
165				Relay:              result.Relay,
166				TransferMode:       result.RelayMode,
167				Reason:             result.RelayReason,
168				BootstrapSuggested: result.BootstrapSuggested,
169				SourceHEAD:         result.SourceHEAD.String(),
170				Batch: BatchSummary{
171					Enabled: result.Batching,
172					Planned: result.PlannedBatchCount,
173					Done:    result.BatchCount,
174				},
175			},
176			Stats:       FromStats(result.Stats),
177			Measurement: FromMeasurement(result.Measurement),
178		}
179		for _, plan := range result.Plans {
180			out.Refs = append(out.Refs, RefResult{
181				Branch:     plan.Branch,
182				SourceRef:  plan.SourceRef.String(),
183				TargetRef:  plan.TargetRef.String(),
184				SourceHash: HashString(plan.SourceHash),
185				TargetHash: HashString(plan.TargetHash),
186				Kind:       RefKind(plan.Kind),
187				Action:     Action(plan.Action),
188				Reason:     plan.Reason,
189			})
190		}
191		return out
192	}
193	
194	func FromStats(stats syncer.Stats) Stats {
195		out := Stats{Enabled: stats.Enabled, Items: make(map[string]*ServiceStats, len(stats.Items))}
196		for key, item := range stats.Items {
197			copyItem := *item
198			out.Items[key] = &ServiceStats{
199				Name:          copyItem.Name,
200				Requests:      copyItem.Requests,
201				RequestBytes:  copyItem.RequestBytes,
202				ResponseBytes: copyItem.ResponseBytes,
203				Wants:         copyItem.Wants,
204				Haves:         copyItem.Haves,
205				Commands:      copyItem.Commands,
206			}
207		}
208		if len(stats.Sides) > 0 {
209			out.Sides = make([]SideBytes, 0, len(stats.Sides))
210			for _, side := range stats.Sides {
211				out.Sides = append(out.Sides, SideBytes{
212					Label:       side.Label,
213					Bytes:       side.Bytes,
214					Display:     side.Display,
215					ActiveNanos: side.ActiveNanos,
216					IdleNanos:   side.IdleNanos,
217				})
218			}
219		}
220		out.ElapsedNanos = stats.ElapsedNanos
221		return out
222	}
223	
224	func FromMeasurement(m syncer.Measurement) Measurement {
225		return Measurement{
226			Enabled:            m.Enabled,
227			ElapsedMillis:      m.ElapsedMillis,
228			PeakAllocBytes:     m.PeakAllocBytes,
229			PeakHeapInuseBytes: m.PeakHeapInuseBytes,
230			TotalAllocBytes:    m.TotalAllocBytes,
231			GCCount:            m.GCCount,
232		}
233	}
234	
235	func HashString(hash plumbing.Hash) string {
236		if hash.IsZero() {
237			return ""
238		}
239		return hash.String()
240	}

1	package gitsync
2	
3	import (
4		"context"
5		"errors"
6	
7		"entire.io/entire/git-sync/internal/internalbridge"
8	)
9	
10	// ProtocolMode controls source-side protocol negotiation.
11	type ProtocolMode string
12	
13	const (
14		ProtocolAuto ProtocolMode = "auto"
15		ProtocolV1   ProtocolMode = "v1"
16		ProtocolV2   ProtocolMode = "v2"
17	)
18	
19	// OperationMode controls high-level sync semantics.
20	type OperationMode string
21	
22	const (
23		ModeSync      OperationMode = "sync"
24		ModeReplicate OperationMode = "replicate"
25	)
26	
27	// Endpoint identifies a remote Git endpoint.
28	type Endpoint struct {
29		URL string `json:"url"`
30	
31		// FollowInfoRefsRedirect, when true, rewrites this endpoint's
32		// effective host to the final URL returned by /info/refs after
33		// HTTP redirects. Subsequent git RPCs (git-upload-pack,
34		// git-receive-pack) then target the redirected host directly.
35		// Matches vanilla git's smart-HTTP behaviour for discovery-aware
36		// servers that 307 /info/refs to a hosting replica.
37		FollowInfoRefsRedirect bool `json:"followInfoRefsRedirect,omitempty"`
38	}
39	
40	// EndpointAuth carries explicit per-request auth and TLS settings.
41	// It is resolved through an AuthProvider rather than embedded in Endpoint so
42	// endpoint identity does not also become the public auth-precedence boundary.
43	type EndpointAuth struct {
44		Username      string `json:"username"`
45		Token         string `json:"token"`
46		BearerToken   string `json:"bearerToken"`
47		SkipTLSVerify bool   `json:"skipTlsVerify"`
48	}
49	
50	// EndpointRole identifies whether auth is being resolved for the source or target.
51	type EndpointRole string
52	
53	const (
54		SourceRole EndpointRole = "source"
55		TargetRole EndpointRole = "target"
56	)
57	
58	// AuthProvider resolves auth for a request endpoint.
59	type AuthProvider interface {
60		AuthFor(ctx context.Context, endpoint Endpoint, role EndpointRole) (EndpointAuth, error)
61	}
62	
63	// StaticAuthProvider returns fixed source and target auth values.
64	type StaticAuthProvider struct {
65		Source EndpointAuth `json:"source"`
66		Target EndpointAuth `json:"target"`
67	}
68	
69	// AuthFor implements AuthProvider.
70	func (p StaticAuthProvider) AuthFor(_ context.Context, _ Endpoint, role EndpointRole) (EndpointAuth, error) { //nolint:unparam // implements AuthProvider interface
71		if role == TargetRole {
72			return p.Target, nil
73		}
74		return p.Source, nil
75	}
76	
77	// RefMapping is an explicit source-to-target ref mapping.
78	type RefMapping struct {
79		Source string `json:"source"`
80		Target string `json:"target"`
81	}
82	
83	// RefScope constrains which refs a request manages. AllRefs broadens scope
84	// to every refs/* on the source (branches, tags, notes, pulls, custom
85	// namespaces) and implies SyncPolicy.IncludeTags. ExcludeRefPrefixes
86	// subtracts namespaces from auto-discovery (useful for trimming
87	// refs/pull/* when mirroring open-source GitHub repos under AllRefs);
88	// explicit Mappings are not subject to it.
89	type RefScope struct {
90		Branches           []string     `json:"branches"`
91		Mappings           []RefMapping `json:"mappings"`
92		AllRefs            bool         `json:"allRefs,omitempty"`
93		ExcludeRefPrefixes []string     `json:"excludeRefPrefixes,omitempty"`
94	}
95	
96	// SyncPolicy controls high-level sync behavior. BestEffort downgrades per-ref
97	// receive-pack rejections to warnings; pack-level failures remain fatal.
98	//
99	// ForceWithLease and ForceBlind both allow non-fast-forward branch updates and
100	// tag retargets; they differ in how the push command's expected-old value is
101	// set. ForceWithLease sends the target tip captured at session start, so
102	// receive-pack rejects updates where the target moved during the run (the
103	// "lease"). ForceBlind sends a zero expected-old, telling receive-pack to
104	// overwrite regardless of the current target value — matching `git push
105	// --force` semantics. The two are mutually exclusive.
106	type SyncPolicy struct {
107		Mode           OperationMode `json:"mode"`
108		IncludeTags    bool          `json:"includeTags"`
109		ForceWithLease bool          `json:"forceWithLease,omitempty"`
110		ForceBlind     bool          `json:"forceBlind,omitempty"`
111		Prune          bool          `json:"prune"`
112		BestEffort     bool          `json:"bestEffort,omitempty"`
113		Protocol       ProtocolMode  `json:"protocol"`
114	}
115	
116	// Validate enforces SyncPolicy invariants at the request edge.
117	func (p SyncPolicy) Validate() error {
118		if p.ForceWithLease && p.ForceBlind {
119			return errors.New("ForceWithLease and ForceBlind are mutually exclusive")
120		}
121		if p.Mode == ModeReplicate && (p.ForceWithLease || p.ForceBlind) {
122			return errors.New("replicate does not support force flags; use sync instead")
123		}
124		return nil
125	}
126	
127	// ProbeRequest inspects source refs and optional target capabilities.
128	type ProbeRequest struct {
129		Source             Endpoint     `json:"source"`
130		Target             *Endpoint    `json:"target"`
131		IncludeTags        bool         `json:"includeTags"`
132		AllRefs            bool         `json:"allRefs,omitempty"`
133		ExcludeRefPrefixes []string     `json:"excludeRefPrefixes,omitempty"`
134		Protocol           ProtocolMode `json:"protocol"`
135		CollectStats       bool         `json:"collectStats"`
136	}
137	
138	// PlanRequest computes ref actions without pushing.
139	type PlanRequest struct {
140		Source       Endpoint   `json:"source"`
141		Target       Endpoint   `json:"target"`
142		Scope        RefScope   `json:"scope"`
143		Policy       SyncPolicy `json:"policy"`
144		CollectStats bool       `json:"collectStats"`
145	}
146	
147	// SyncRequest executes a sync between two remotes.
148	type SyncRequest struct {
149		Source       Endpoint   `json:"source"`
150		Target       Endpoint   `json:"target"`
151		Scope        RefScope   `json:"scope"`
152		Policy       SyncPolicy `json:"policy"`
153		CollectStats bool       `json:"collectStats"`
154	}
155

159		RefKindBranch RefKind = internalbridge.RefKindBranch
160		RefKindTag    RefKind = internalbridge.RefKindTag
161		RefKindOther  RefKind = internalbridge.RefKindOther
162	)
163	
164	type Action = internalbridge.Action
165	
166	const (
167		ActionCreate Action = internalbridge.ActionCreate
168		ActionUpdate Action = internalbridge.ActionUpdate
169		ActionDelete Action = internalbridge.ActionDelete
170		ActionSkip   Action = internalbridge.ActionSkip
171		ActionBlock  Action = internalbridge.ActionBlock
172		ActionWarn   Action = internalbridge.ActionWarn
173	)

1	package gitsync
2	
3	import "entire.io/entire/git-sync/internal/gitproto"
4	
5	// ErrTargetRefMoved is returned (wrapped) by Sync and Replicate when a push was
6	// rejected because the target ref changed concurrently between this run's plan
7	// and its push — a benign, retryable compare-and-swap / lease miss, not a real
8	// failure. Test for it with errors.Is(err, gitsync.ErrTargetRefMoved).
9	//
10	// On the default push path the concrete error in the chain is a
11	// *RefRejectedError (reachable with errors.As). One case does NOT carry that
12	// concrete type: a BestEffort run with ForceWithLease escalates a lease miss
13	// through a plain wrapped error that still satisfies errors.Is(ErrTargetRefMoved)
14	// but is not a *RefRejectedError. So prefer errors.Is when you only need the
15	// cause, and treat a successful errors.As(*RefRejectedError) as best-effort.
16	//
17	// This is the supported way to distinguish a racing concurrent push from a
18	// genuine push failure; prefer it over inspecting the error message text, which
19	// is free-form and server-specific.
20	var ErrTargetRefMoved = gitproto.ErrTargetRefMoved
21	
22	// RefRejectedError is a single per-ref "ng" status returned by the target's
23	// receive-pack report-status, reachable with errors.As. Ref is the rejected ref
24	// and Reason is the raw server reason text. Rejections that are concurrent
25	// target-ref moves also satisfy errors.Is(err, ErrTargetRefMoved).
26	type RefRejectedError = gitproto.RefRejectedError
27

# git-sync public API surface: simplification report

Module `entire.io/entire/git-sync`. Public (frozen) surface = root package `gitsync` + `unstable/`. Everything under `internal/` (including `internal/internalbridge`) can change freely. All findings below preserve the public API.

## How the layers actually wire together (verified)

Root `gitsync.Client` path (client.go):
`gitsync.SyncRequest` → `bridgeEndpoint/bridgeEndpointAuth/bridgeScope/bridgePolicy` (client.go:191-233) → `internalbridge.Endpoint/EndpointAuth/RefScope/SyncPolicy` mirror types → `internalbridge.SyncConfig` (config.go:75) → `syncer.Config` → `internalbridge.Run` (config.go:105, pure unwrap) → `syncer.Run` → `syncer.Result` → `internalbridge.FromSyncResult` (model.go:151) → `gitsync.SyncResult` (alias of `internalbridge.SyncResult`).

`unstable.Client` path (unstable/client.go): `gitsync` types → `syncer.Config` **built directly inline** (buildSyncConfig, unstable/client.go:250), calls `syncer.Run` directly, returns raw `syncer.Result`. It touches internalbridge for only two helpers: `ToSyncerEndpoint` and `ToValidationMappings` (unstable/client.go:377,399).

That contrast is the core of the report: **unstable already proves the root package does not need the internalbridge config layer.**

---

## Finding 1 (HIGH) — internalbridge/config.go is a redundant second mapping layer; delete it

**Files:** internal/internalbridge/config.go (whole file), client.go:84-133, 191-233.

The mirror config types in config.go — `Endpoint` (30), `EndpointAuth` (35), `RefScope` (42), `SyncPolicy` (49), `RefMapping` (25), `ProtocolMode`/`OperationMode` (11-12) and the 5 constants (18-23) — duplicate the root `gitsync` types in types.go **field-for-field**. This forces a double conversion in the hot path: root type → bridge mirror type → `syncer.Config`. The `bridgeEndpoint/bridgeEndpointAuth/bridgeScope/bridgePolicy` funcs in client.go (191-233) exist only to feed those mirror types.

**Why it's slop:** the intermediate types carry zero information the root types don't. Proof it's unnecessary: `unstable/client.go:250-289` builds `syncer.Config` directly from `gitsync.*` request fields with no mirror layer. The root client can do exactly the same.

Per-function verdict for config.go:
- `ProbeConfig` (59) / `SyncConfig` (75) — thin `syncer.Config` builders. Apply two trivial defaults (protocol "auto", `MaterializedMaxObjects`, mode "sync"). **Duplicated** by unstable's buildProbeConfig/buildSyncConfig. Fold into root.
- `Probe` (97) / `Run` (105) — **pure pass-through** to `syncer.Probe`/`syncer.Run`, only unwrapping `cfg.raw`. No transform, no defaults, no validation. Pure ceremony.
- `Config` struct (14) with unexported `raw syncer.Config` — wrapper that hides `syncer.Config` from nobody (internalbridge is itself internal). Pure ceremony.
- `ToSyncerEndpoint` (113) — **real** field mapping, used by root and unstable. Keep (move to `internal/syncer`).
- `ToValidationMappings` (138) — **real** `[]RefMapping`→`[]validation.RefMapping` mapping, used by root and unstable. Keep (move to `internal/syncer` or `internal/validation`).
- `protocolString` (124) / `operationModeString` (131) — default helpers, **duplicated verbatim** in unstable/client.go:362,369 (see Finding 5).

**Safe simplification:** have the root client build `syncer.Config` directly (mirror unstable's approach), delete config.go's mirror types + `Config` wrapper + `ProbeConfig`/`SyncConfig`/`Probe`/`Run`, and relocate the two real helpers (`ToSyncerEndpoint`, `ToValidationMappings`) to `internal/syncer`. Root and unstable both already import `syncer`, so there is no import cycle (syncer never imports root). No public symbol changes. config.go has **no test file** (only model_test.go exists), so nothing to migrate.

---

## Finding 2 (MEDIUM-HIGH) — internalbridge/model.go is legitimate, but its placement + alias indirection is avoidable, and it leaks an unaliased type

**File:** internal/internalbridge/model.go, types.go:156-186.

Unlike config.go, model.go does **real work** and is the actual public result contract. `FromSyncResult` (151) is a genuine transform: it regroups the flat `syncer.Result` into the nested public shape (`Counts`, `Execution`, `Execution.Batch`), renames fields (`Pushed`→`Counts.Applied`, `RelayMode`→`TransferMode`, `RelayReason`→`Reason`), **drops** `TempRefs`, and converts `plumbing.Hash`/`ReferenceName`→string. `FromProbeResult` (131) and `FromStats` (194) likewise deep-copy and stringify hashes to keep `plumbing` out of the public API. These must be kept.

Per-function verdict for model.go: `FromProbeResult`, `FromSyncResult`, `FromStats`, `HashString` = real transforms, keep. `FromMeasurement` (224) = pure field-for-field copy between identically-shaped structs, borderline (could be a one-line struct conversion but harmless).

Two issues:

**2a. Placement.** model.go is imported by **only the root package** (verified: unstable uses none of model.go). Yet the types physically live in `internal/internalbridge` and are surfaced through 20 lines of `type X = internalbridge.X` aliases (types.go:156-186). Moving these result types + the `From*` converters into the root `gitsync` package deletes the entire alias block and the second package, with **zero external API change** (external users only ever write `gitsync.ProbeResult`; the alias target is internal and unnameable to them).

**2b. Public type leak — `SideBytes` is not aliased.** `internalbridge.Stats.Sides` is `[]SideBytes` (model.go:68), and `gitsync.Stats = internalbridge.Stats`. But there is **no `gitsync.SideBytes` alias** (grep of types.go confirms). So an external caller can read `result.Stats.Sides` but cannot name the element type — it resolves to the internal `internalbridge.SideBytes`. This is a real hole in the published surface. Fix: add `type SideBytes = internalbridge.SideBytes` (or, after the 2a move, define it in root).

---

## Finding 3 (MEDIUM) — mappings validated up to 3×, protocol normalized 2×

**Files:** client.go:169,172,185; internal/syncer/syncer.go:647,659; internal/planner/planner.go:66.

Root `validateSyncFields` (client.go:156) calls `validation.NormalizeProtocolMode` (169) and `validation.ValidateMappings` (172). The same request then reaches `syncer.Run`, which calls `validation.NormalizeProtocolMode` (syncer.go:647) and `validation.ValidateMappings` (syncer.go:659) again, and `internal/planner` calls `validation.ValidateMappings` a **third** time (planner.go:66). `ProbeRequest.Validate` (client.go:185) also re-normalizes protocol that syncer will re-normalize.

**Why it's slop:** the root edge-validation of protocol + mappings is fully redundant with syncer's internal validation. Note `unstable.Client` does **not** do this edge validation (it relies on syncer) — so the stable client is the outlier.

What root validation *uniquely* provides and must keep: the "source/target URL required" checks (client.go:157-161,179), `SyncPolicy.Validate()` (force-flag mutual exclusion, types.go:117), and `validateOperationMode` (client.go:235). Conversely, root does **not** call `validation.ValidateEndpoints` (same-repo check) at the edge — only syncer does (syncer.go:669) — an asymmetry worth noting.

**Safe simplification (behavior-preserving caveat):** drop the redundant `NormalizeProtocolMode`/`ValidateMappings` calls from client.go edge validation and let syncer be authoritative. Caveat: this changes error *wrapping* (a bad mapping would surface as `"sync: ..."` instead of `"validate mappings: ..."`), so if fast-fail-with-specific-prefix at the edge is a deliberate UX contract, leave it but document that it's intentional duplication. Lowest-risk win here is just consolidating: root validates policy/mode/URLs; syncer owns protocol+mappings+endpoints.

---

## Finding 4 (LOW-MEDIUM) — duplicated helpers between unstable and root

**Files:** unstable/client.go:362-374 vs internal/internalbridge/config.go:124-136; unstable/client.go:391-400 vs client.go:248-257.

- `protocolString` and `operationModeString` are byte-for-byte identical in `unstable/client.go` (362,369) and `internalbridge/config.go` (124,131). Additionally `protocolString`'s "empty → auto" logic overlaps `validation.NormalizeProtocolMode` (validation.go:87). Three copies of "default protocol to auto."
- `validationMappings` (root client.go:248) and `unstable.validationMappings` (unstable/client.go:391) are two spellings of the same `[]…RefMapping → []validation.RefMapping` conversion, both ultimately reaching `internalbridge.ToValidationMappings`.

**Safe simplification:** after Finding 1 relocates `ToSyncerEndpoint`/`ToValidationMappings` into `syncer`, also expose a single `syncer`-level protocol/mode default helper (or reuse `validation.NormalizeProtocolMode`) and have both clients call it. Internal-only, no API impact.

---

## Finding 5 (LOW) — redundant / dead exported aliases

- `RefPlan` — defined twice (`internalbridge.RefPlan = RefResult` at model.go:40, re-aliased `gitsync.RefPlan` at types.go:176) and **consumed nowhere** in the repo (grep: only the two definition lines). It is a published alias for `RefResult` with no producer or consumer. Likely dead; if external compatibility is a concern keep it, otherwise drop.
- `PlanResult` — `internalbridge.PlanResult = SyncResult` (model.go:129), `gitsync.PlanResult = SyncResult` (types.go:186). It is literally `SyncResult` under a second name, used only as the return type of `Client.Plan`. Harmless but pure naming duplication; `Plan` could just return `SyncResult`.
- `ProtocolV1`/`ProtocolV2` (types.go:15-16) have no in-repo consumer (only `ProtocolAuto` is used). Published, so plausibly used externally — flag only, do not remove.
- Note: all root result aliases (`RefKind`, `Action*`, `RefResult`, `ProbeResult`, `SyncResult`, etc.) show 0 `gitsync.`-qualified in-repo uses — expected, because cmd/ consumes `unstable` (raw syncer types) and the root package uses them bare. These are legitimate published API; not dead.

---

## Finding 6 (LOW) — docs drift

- **docs/architecture.md:91-118 ("Package Model") omits `internal/internalbridge` entirely**, even though it currently holds the public result-type contract. Line 116 attributes "result shaping" to `internal/syncer`, but the stable public reshaping (`FromSyncResult`, the `Counts`/`Execution`/`Batch` grouping) lives in internalbridge, not syncer. If Finding 2a is applied (move model.go into root), this doc becomes accurate automatically; otherwise it should mention internalbridge.
- doc.go and example_test.go are **accurate** against the current API: `example_test.go` uses `New`, `StaticAuthProvider`, `SyncRequest`, `RefScope`, `SyncPolicy`, `ProtocolAuto` — all still present with matching fields. doc.go's description ("typed probe, plan, sync requests"; batch sizing/heap measurement kept out of stable surface) matches reality (those live in `unstable.AdvancedOptions`). No staleness found.
- unstable/doc.go accurately describes the `Bootstrap`/`Fetch`/advanced-knobs surface.

---

## Recommended order of execution
1. **Finding 1** (delete config.go mirror layer; build `syncer.Config` directly in root) — biggest reduction, removes ~90 lines of mirror types + `bridge*` funcs + pass-throughs, unifies root with the already-simpler unstable path.
2. **Finding 2a + 2b** (move model.go result types/converters into root package, drop the alias block, add the missing `SideBytes` export) — after this, `internal/internalbridge` disappears entirely (its last two helpers moved to `syncer` in step 1), and the `SideBytes` public leak is closed.
3. **Finding 4** (dedupe protocol/mode/mapping helpers).
4. **Finding 3** (decide edge-vs-core validation ownership — needs a product call on error-message contract).
5. **Findings 5/6** (drop `RefPlan`/`PlanResult` naming duplication if external compat allows; fix architecture.md).

Net effect: `internal/internalbridge` (config.go + model.go, ~11.7 KB) can be eliminated, the 20-line alias block in types.go removed, and the root client made structurally identical to the already-shipping unstable client — with no change to any symbol an external importer can name.</result>

# Simplification / de-slop report: `cmd/git-sync` + `cmd/git-sync-bench`

All findings verified with grep/reads. Ranked by impact × confidence. No feature/flag/CLI-behavior changes in any proposed fix.

## Tier 1 — high impact, high confidence

### 1. Dead adapter + unnecessary interface conversion in sha256convert
- **Where:** `cmd/git-sync/internal/sha256convert/sha256convert.go:955` (call site), `:971-984` (`normalizeAuth`), `:986-993` (`authAdapter` type + method).
- **What:** `normalizeAuth` type-switches an `auth.Method` to return a `gitproto.AuthMethod`, wrapping "other" implementations in `authAdapter`.
- **Why it's slop:** `internal/auth.Method` (`internal/auth/auth.go:19`) and `internal/gitproto.AuthMethod` (`internal/gitproto/smarthttp.go:178`) are byte-for-byte identical interfaces (`Authorizer(req *http.Request) error`), so an `auth.Method` value is directly assignable to a `gitproto.AuthMethod` parameter — no conversion needed. Worse, `auth.Resolve → explicitAuth` (`internal/auth/auth.go:36-51`) only ever returns `*transporthttp.TokenAuth`, `*transporthttp.BasicAuth`, or `nil`. Both concrete types are returned directly by the type switch, so the `authAdapter` fallback branch is **unreachable dead code**.
- **Fix:** Delete `normalizeAuth` and `authAdapter` entirely; pass `authMethod` directly: `gitproto.NewHTTPConnWithClient(ep, "source", authMethod, httpClient)`. `NewHTTPConnWithClient` already handles a nil auth, and a nil `auth.Method` converts to a nil `gitproto.AuthMethod`. Removes ~23 lines.

### 2. convert-sha256 re-implements the shared source-auth flag helper
- **Where:** `cmd/git-sync/convert_sha256.go:77-83`.
- **What:** Manually registers `--source-token`, `--source-username`, `--source-bearer-token`, `--source-insecure-skip-tls-verify`.
- **Why it's slop:** This is a line-for-line copy of `addSourceAuth` (`flags.go:27-34`) — same flag names, same env defaults (`GITSYNC_SOURCE_USERNAME`→`git`, `GITSYNC_SOURCE_TOKEN`, etc.), same usage strings, same `addSecretFlag` secret-leak protection. `req.SourceAuth` is a `gitsync.EndpointAuth` (`sha256convert.go:66`), exactly what `addSourceAuth` takes.
- **Fix:** Replace the 7 lines with `addSourceAuth(cmd, &req.SourceAuth)`. Behavior/help text identical. (The `--source-url`/`--source-follow-info-refs-redirect` pair can't reuse `addSourceEndpoint` without changing `Request` to embed a `gitsync.Endpoint`; leave those as-is or note as a lower-confidence follow-up.)

### 3. Ref-mapping parse loop copy-pasted across subcommands
- **Where:** `cmd/git-sync/syncplan.go:58-67` and `cmd/git-sync/bootstrap.go:40-49` (identical); a third copy in `cmd/git-sync-bench/main.go:142-151`.
- **What:** `for _, raw := range mappings { ParseMapping → append gitsync.RefMapping{Source,Target} }`.
- **Why it's slop:** Byte-identical logic in two files of the same `main` package (bench is a separate package, so only the first two are cheaply shareable).
- **Fix:** Add `func parseMappings(raw []string) ([]gitsync.RefMapping, error)` to `flags.go`; call it from both `syncplan.go` and `bootstrap.go`. The adjacent `if branches != "" { req.Scope.Branches = splitCSV(branches) }` (also in syncplan/bootstrap/fetch) can fold into the same scope-assembly helper.

## Tier 2 — medium impact / cleanups

### 4. Redundant intermediate named types for flag wrappers
- **Where:** `cmd/git-sync/flags.go:182-186` (`type protocolMode …`, `type operationMode …`, `type protocolModeFlag protocolMode`, `type operationModeFlag operationMode`), plus conversions at `:129`, `:196`, `:206`, `:216-220`.
- **What:** Two-level type ladders where `protocolModeFlag`→`protocolMode`→`gitsync.ProtocolMode` (a `string`), likewise for operation mode.
- **Why it's slop:** The middle types `protocolMode`/`operationMode` are never used for anything except being converted through — grep shows no standalone use. They produce triple conversions like `protocolModeFlag(protocolMode(gitsync.ProtocolMode(mode)))` (`:196`).
- **Fix:** Collapse to `type protocolModeFlag gitsync.ProtocolMode` and `type operationModeFlag gitsync.OperationMode`; conversion at `:196` becomes `protocolModeFlag(mode)`. Have `defaultOperationMode` return `gitsync.OperationMode` (or `operationModeFlag`) directly. Pure type-def cleanup, behavior-preserving.

### 5. Version-line format string duplicated
- **Where:** `cmd/git-sync/root.go:30-31` (`SetVersionTemplate`) and `cmd/git-sync/version.go:15-16` (`version` subcommand).
- **What:** `"git-sync %s (commit %s, built %s)\n"` with the same three `versioninfo` args in both places.
- **Why it's slop:** Two copies that must stay in lockstep; `--version` and `version` print identically.
- **Fix:** Add `func String() string` to `internal/versioninfo` returning the formatted line; both call sites use it. (Keep both entry points — dropping the subcommand would change the CLI.)

### 6. `uniqueStrings` reimplements `slices.Compact`
- **Where:** `cmd/git-sync-bench/main.go:381-395`.
- **What:** Sort + manual consecutive-dedup loop.
- **Why it's slop:** `slices` is already imported (`main.go:12`). The whole body is `slices.Sort(input); return slices.Compact(input)` (with the empty→nil guard preserved by returning `input` which is nil when empty).
- **Fix:** Reduce to two lines via `slices.Compact`.

## Tier 3 — low impact, high confidence

### 7. probe re-implements the target-endpoint flag helper
- **Where:** `cmd/git-sync/probe.go:57-60` vs `flags.go:20-25` (`addTargetEndpoint`).
- **What:** Manually registers `--target-url` (usage "optional target repository URL") and `--target-follow-info-refs-redirect` (same `GITSYNC_TARGET_FOLLOW_INFO_REFS_REDIRECT` env).
- **Why it's slop:** Near-duplicate of `addTargetEndpoint`. Not a clean swap because probe's target is optional (`req.Target` is a `*gitsync.Endpoint` built post-parse from local vars), and the usage string intentionally differs. Lower priority; flag as "duplication, refactor only if `ProbeRequest` grows a non-pointer target."

### 8. Orphaned doc comment for `resolveMessageRef`
- **Where:** `cmd/git-sync/internal/sha256convert/sha256convert.go:1553-1564`.
- **What:** The doc block "`resolveMessageRef` classifies a hex prefix…" (1553-1558) is immediately followed by the `resolveCacheEntry` type + its own comment (1559-1564), then the function at `:1566`.
- **Why it's slop:** Go attaches the last contiguous comment before a declaration to that declaration, so the `resolveMessageRef` prose becomes `resolveCacheEntry`'s godoc and the function ends up undocumented. A `resolveCacheEntry` type declaration was inserted between a comment and its function.
- **Fix:** Move the `resolveCacheEntry` type (and its comment) above the `resolveMessageRef` doc block, so the `resolveMessageRef` comment sits directly on its function.

### 9. `splitCSV` duplicated across the two binaries
- **Where:** `cmd/git-sync/flags.go:170-180` and `cmd/git-sync-bench/main.go:465-475` (identical).
- **Why it's slop:** Same helper in two `main` packages.
- **Fix:** Only worth it if you already have a shared internal util package (e.g. move to `internal/…`); otherwise leave — cross-`main`-package sharing costs a new import for a 10-line function. Low priority.

### 10. bench `SourceURL` mirrored in report and config
- **Where:** `cmd/git-sync-bench/main.go:64` (`benchmarkReport.SourceURL`) and `:74` (`benchmarkConfig.SourceURL`), both tagged `json:"sourceUrl"`.
- **What:** JSON output carries `sourceUrl` at top level and again under `config.sourceUrl`, always the same value.
- **Why it's slop:** Field mirroring. `report.SourceURL` is only read by `printTextReport` (`:429`); `config.SourceURL` drives execution.
- **Fix (optional, JSON-changing so gate carefully):** drop `benchmarkReport.SourceURL`, have `printTextReport` read `report.Config.SourceURL`. Note: this removes a top-level JSON key, so only do it if that JSON shape isn't a consumed contract — otherwise leave it.

## Checked and deliberately NOT flagged (to save you re-investigating)

- **`sha256convert.Run` re-validates empty `SourceURL`/`TargetDir` (`sha256convert.go:223-228`) after the CLI's `resolveConvertSHA256Args` already did.** Not slop: `Run` is an exported package entry point called directly in tests (`sha256convert_test.go:765, 908, 999, 1072, 1090, 1395`); the guards are legitimate defense for non-CLI callers.
- **Submodule guard duplicated in `translateTree` (`:1267-1276`) vs `discoverReachable` (`:1146-1163`).** It's genuinely unreachable given discovery runs first, but the author documented it as an intentional belt-and-braces guard and it's one comparison per tree entry. Borderline; only remove if you want to trust discovery as the single gate — I'd leave it.
- **No TODO/FIXME/XXX, no parsed-but-unused flags, no read-but-ignored env vars** found in the `cmd` trees (grep clean). `--force`/`legacyForce` is intentionally-retained-and-hidden (errors out with migration guidance), not dead.
- **`stripSignatures` and `previewJoin`** are already well-factored (each used by two call sites) — no action.

**Highest ROI:** #1 (dead code, ~23 lines), #2 (clean helper reuse), #3 (dedup across subcommands). All three are behavior-preserving with high confidence.</result>

# git-sync public API surface: simplification report

Module `entire.io/entire/git-sync`. Public (frozen) surface = root package `gitsync` + `unstable/`. Everything under `internal/` (including `internal/internalbridge`) can change freely. All findings below preserve the public API.

## How the layers actually wire together (verified)

Root `gitsync.Client` path (client.go):
`gitsync.SyncRequest` → `bridgeEndpoint/bridgeEndpointAuth/bridgeScope/bridgePolicy` (client.go:191-233) → `internalbridge.Endpoint/EndpointAuth/RefScope/SyncPolicy` mirror types → `internalbridge.SyncConfig` (config.go:75) → `syncer.Config` → `internalbridge.Run` (config.go:105, pure unwrap) → `syncer.Run` → `syncer.Result` → `internalbridge.FromSyncResult` (model.go:151) → `gitsync.SyncResult` (alias of `internalbridge.SyncResult`).

`unstable.Client` path (unstable/client.go): `gitsync` types → `syncer.Config` **built directly inline** (buildSyncConfig, unstable/client.go:250), calls `syncer.Run` directly, returns raw `syncer.Result`. It touches internalbridge for only two helpers: `ToSyncerEndpoint` and `ToValidationMappings` (unstable/client.go:377,399).

That contrast is the core of the report: **unstable already proves the root package does not need the internalbridge config layer.**

---

## Finding 1 (HIGH) — internalbridge/config.go is a redundant second mapping layer; delete it

**Files:** internal/internalbridge/config.go (whole file), client.go:84-133, 191-233.

The mirror config types in config.go — `Endpoint` (30), `EndpointAuth` (35), `RefScope` (42), `SyncPolicy` (49), `RefMapping` (25), `ProtocolMode`/`OperationMode` (11-12) and the 5 constants (18-23) — duplicate the root `gitsync` types in types.go **field-for-field**. This forces a double conversion in the hot path: root type → bridge mirror type → `syncer.Config`. The `bridgeEndpoint/bridgeEndpointAuth/bridgeScope/bridgePolicy` funcs in client.go (191-233) exist only to feed those mirror types.

**Why it's slop:** the intermediate types carry zero information the root types don't. Proof it's unnecessary: `unstable/client.go:250-289` builds `syncer.Config` directly from `gitsync.*` request fields with no mirror layer. The root client can do exactly the same.

Per-function verdict for config.go:
- `ProbeConfig` (59) / `SyncConfig` (75) — thin `syncer.Config` builders. Apply two trivial defaults (protocol "auto", `MaterializedMaxObjects`, mode "sync"). **Duplicated** by unstable's buildProbeConfig/buildSyncConfig. Fold into root.
- `Probe` (97) / `Run` (105) — **pure pass-through** to `syncer.Probe`/`syncer.Run`, only unwrapping `cfg.raw`. No transform, no defaults, no validation. Pure ceremony.
- `Config` struct (14) with unexported `raw syncer.Config` — wrapper that hides `syncer.Config` from nobody (internalbridge is itself internal). Pure ceremony.
- `ToSyncerEndpoint` (113) — **real** field mapping, used by root and unstable. Keep (move to `internal/syncer`).
- `ToValidationMappings` (138) — **real** `[]RefMapping`→`[]validation.RefMapping` mapping, used by root and unstable. Keep (move to `internal/syncer` or `internal/validation`).
- `protocolString` (124) / `operationModeString` (131) — default helpers, **duplicated verbatim** in unstable/client.go:362,369 (see Finding 5).

**Safe simplification:** have the root client build `syncer.Config` directly (mirror unstable's approach), delete config.go's mirror types + `Config` wrapper + `ProbeConfig`/`SyncConfig`/`Probe`/`Run`, and relocate the two real helpers (`ToSyncerEndpoint`, `ToValidationMappings`) to `internal/syncer`. Root and unstable both already import `syncer`, so there is no import cycle (syncer never imports root). No public symbol changes. config.go has **no test file** (only model_test.go exists), so nothing to migrate.

---

## Finding 2 (MEDIUM-HIGH) — internalbridge/model.go is legitimate, but its placement + alias indirection is avoidable, and it leaks an unaliased type

**File:** internal/internalbridge/model.go, types.go:156-186.

Unlike config.go, model.go does **real work** and is the actual public result contract. `FromSyncResult` (151) is a genuine transform: it regroups the flat `syncer.Result` into the nested public shape (`Counts`, `Execution`, `Execution.Batch`), renames fields (`Pushed`→`Counts.Applied`, `RelayMode`→`TransferMode`, `RelayReason`→`Reason`), **drops** `TempRefs`, and converts `plumbing.Hash`/`ReferenceName`→string. `FromProbeResult` (131) and `FromStats` (194) likewise deep-copy and stringify hashes to keep `plumbing` out of the public API. These must be kept.

Per-function verdict for model.go: `FromProbeResult`, `FromSyncResult`, `FromStats`, `HashString` = real transforms, keep. `FromMeasurement` (224) = pure field-for-field copy between identically-shaped structs, borderline (could be a one-line struct conversion but harmless).

Two issues:

**2a. Placement.** model.go is imported by **only the root package** (verified: unstable uses none of model.go). Yet the types physically live in `internal/internalbridge` and are surfaced through 20 lines of `type X = internalbridge.X` aliases (types.go:156-186). Moving these result types + the `From*` converters into the root `gitsync` package deletes the entire alias block and the second package, with **zero external API change** (external users only ever write `gitsync.ProbeResult`; the alias target is internal and unnameable to them).

**2b. Public type leak — `SideBytes` is not aliased.** `internalbridge.Stats.Sides` is `[]SideBytes` (model.go:68), and `gitsync.Stats = internalbridge.Stats`. But there is **no `gitsync.SideBytes` alias** (grep of types.go confirms). So an external caller can read `result.Stats.Sides` but cannot name the element type — it resolves to the internal `internalbridge.SideBytes`. This is a real hole in the published surface. Fix: add `type SideBytes = internalbridge.SideBytes` (or, after the 2a move, define it in root).

---

## Finding 3 (MEDIUM) — mappings validated up to 3×, protocol normalized 2×

**Files:** client.go:169,172,185; internal/syncer/syncer.go:647,659; internal/planner/planner.go:66.

Root `validateSyncFields` (client.go:156) calls `validation.NormalizeProtocolMode` (169) and `validation.ValidateMappings` (172). The same request then reaches `syncer.Run`, which calls `validation.NormalizeProtocolMode` (syncer.go:647) and `validation.ValidateMappings` (syncer.go:659) again, and `internal/planner` calls `validation.ValidateMappings` a **third** time (planner.go:66). `ProbeRequest.Validate` (client.go:185) also re-normalizes protocol that syncer will re-normalize.

**Why it's slop:** the root edge-validation of protocol + mappings is fully redundant with syncer's internal validation. Note `unstable.Client` does **not** do this edge validation (it relies on syncer) — so the stable client is the outlier.

What root validation *uniquely* provides and must keep: the "source/target URL required" checks (client.go:157-161,179), `SyncPolicy.Validate()` (force-flag mutual exclusion, types.go:117), and `validateOperationMode` (client.go:235). Conversely, root does **not** call `validation.ValidateEndpoints` (same-repo check) at the edge — only syncer does (syncer.go:669) — an asymmetry worth noting.

**Safe simplification (behavior-preserving caveat):** drop the redundant `NormalizeProtocolMode`/`ValidateMappings` calls from client.go edge validation and let syncer be authoritative. Caveat: this changes error *wrapping* (a bad mapping would surface as `"sync: ..."` instead of `"validate mappings: ..."`), so if fast-fail-with-specific-prefix at the edge is a deliberate UX contract, leave it but document that it's intentional duplication. Lowest-risk win here is just consolidating: root validates policy/mode/URLs; syncer owns protocol+mappings+endpoints.

---

## Finding 4 (LOW-MEDIUM) — duplicated helpers between unstable and root

**Files:** unstable/client.go:362-374 vs internal/internalbridge/config.go:124-136; unstable/client.go:391-400 vs client.go:248-257.

- `protocolString` and `operationModeString` are byte-for-byte identical in `unstable/client.go` (362,369) and `internalbridge/config.go` (124,131). Additionally `protocolString`'s "empty → auto" logic overlaps `validation.NormalizeProtocolMode` (validation.go:87). Three copies of "default protocol to auto."
- `validationMappings` (root client.go:248) and `unstable.validationMappings` (unstable/client.go:391) are two spellings of the same `[]…RefMapping → []validation.RefMapping` conversion, both ultimately reaching `internalbridge.ToValidationMappings`.

**Safe simplification:** after Finding 1 relocates `ToSyncerEndpoint`/`ToValidationMappings` into `syncer`, also expose a single `syncer`-level protocol/mode default helper (or reuse `validation.NormalizeProtocolMode`) and have both clients call it. Internal-only, no API impact.

---

## Finding 5 (LOW) — redundant / dead exported aliases

- `RefPlan` — defined twice (`internalbridge.RefPlan = RefResult` at model.go:40, re-aliased `gitsync.RefPlan` at types.go:176) and **consumed nowhere** in the repo (grep: only the two definition lines). It is a published alias for `RefResult` with no producer or consumer. Likely dead; if external compatibility is a concern keep it, otherwise drop.
- `PlanResult` — `internalbridge.PlanResult = SyncResult` (model.go:129), `gitsync.PlanResult = SyncResult` (types.go:186). It is literally `SyncResult` under a second name, used only as the return type of `Client.Plan`. Harmless but pure naming duplication; `Plan` could just return `SyncResult`.
- `ProtocolV1`/`ProtocolV2` (types.go:15-16) have no in-repo consumer (only `ProtocolAuto` is used). Published, so plausibly used externally — flag only, do not remove.
- Note: all root result aliases (`RefKind`, `Action*`, `RefResult`, `ProbeResult`, `SyncResult`, etc.) show 0 `gitsync.`-qualified in-repo uses — expected, because cmd/ consumes `unstable` (raw syncer types) and the root package uses them bare. These are legitimate published API; not dead.

---

## Finding 6 (LOW) — docs drift

- **docs/architecture.md:91-118 ("Package Model") omits `internal/internalbridge` entirely**, even though it currently holds the public result-type contract. Line 116 attributes "result shaping" to `internal/syncer`, but the stable public reshaping (`FromSyncResult`, the `Counts`/`Execution`/`Batch` grouping) lives in internalbridge, not syncer. If Finding 2a is applied (move model.go into root), this doc becomes accurate automatically; otherwise it should mention internalbridge.
- doc.go and example_test.go are **accurate** against the current API: `example_test.go` uses `New`, `StaticAuthProvider`, `SyncRequest`, `RefScope`, `SyncPolicy`, `ProtocolAuto` — all still present with matching fields. doc.go's description ("typed probe, plan, sync requests"; batch sizing/heap measurement kept out of stable surface) matches reality (those live in `unstable.AdvancedOptions`). No staleness found.
- unstable/doc.go accurately describes the `Bootstrap`/`Fetch`/advanced-knobs surface.

---

## Recommended order of execution
1. **Finding 1** (delete config.go mirror layer; build `syncer.Config` directly in root) — biggest reduction, removes ~90 lines of mirror types + `bridge*` funcs + pass-throughs, unifies root with the already-simpler unstable path.
2. **Finding 2a + 2b** (move model.go result types/converters into root package, drop the alias block, add the missing `SideBytes` export) — after this, `internal/internalbridge` disappears entirely (its last two helpers moved to `syncer` in step 1), and the `SideBytes` public leak is closed.
3. **Finding 4** (dedupe protocol/mode/mapping helpers).
4. **Finding 3** (decide edge-vs-core validation ownership — needs a product call on error-message contract).
5. **Findings 5/6** (drop `RefPlan`/`PlanResult` naming duplication if external compat allows; fix architecture.md).

Net effect: `internal/internalbridge` (config.go + model.go, ~11.7 KB) can be eliminated, the 20-line alias block in types.go removed, and the root client made structurally identical to the already-shipping unstable client — with no change to any symbol an external importer can name.</result>
